location: West and NW faces on Great Northern and Grant
West and Northwest faces of Great Northern and Grant
Avalanche Summary: Observed many smaller Wet Loose piles around the fields of rollerballs.
Avalanche size is estimated. The debris pile was out of sight and it was a long way away. I assume this failed on the early February facet/crust.
Two D1.5 wet loose slides slid on March 14, and one slid today, March 15.

Date and time of occurrence unknown but forced to include a date of occurrence. Image from Milk River/Cut Bank divide (closest observation point). No other details since we viewed it from afar. Visible from Hwy 89 before Looking Glass junction. D size estimated (D3). Runout not visible.
wet loose on steep rocky terrain at 6800'. most likely triggered during afternoon warmup
We got a few very small slabs to pop on wind whales during the ascent.
WL on SW aspects. all apeared to have failed yesterday afternoon during warmup.
multiple wet loose released on westerly aspect most likely during PM warmup. located near small steep convexities
Looks like it was likely triggered sometime in past 48 and likely by a chunk of falling cornice from above
Partner kicked off a small (D1) wet slide at the bottom of the skook chutes around 5500', SE aspect at 13:00 on our descent.

A small wet loose avalanche was ski-cut by a rider within the party. Given the warming and sunny conditions and the chance of having a wet/dry loose avalanche step down into a weaker persistent layer,  our party decided to descend via a more tree-protected route. Standing above a brief convex opening, the rider cut across the slope at a safe position and released the small slide.

The slide started relatively small but picked up momentum as it traveled through trees and over a small rock band. Chances of being buried were negligible, but going for a ride through the trees and rock band could have potentially been consequential. 

Would characterize the snow on the verge of wet loose from dry loose.

The second snowmobiler on the slope triggered a wind slab near the ridgetop that stepped down to the Feb 3 facet/crust layer.  We estimated the slab to be less than 30 cm thick where it was triggered just below the ridgeline. The avalanche propagated about 350' in both directions wrapping around the bowl. The deepest part of the crown was 80 cm.

While skiing down chicken bones proper I came across this slide that was set off earlier in the day.  Appears to have been skier triggered (looked like two skiers) at the convexity of the skiers left steeper “chute.”  Looked to be a D2 on the storm layer.  Crown varied from 6” to 1’ foot deep indicating wind loading as well.
First and second riders on slope triggered dry loose avalanches that ran approximately 75’ and 10’ wide, entrained surface snow. No one caught or carried, both rode out of slides without incident.

One recent and one old persistent slab avalanche observed in steep, alpine terrain near Baptiste Peak in the Flathead Range. 

Also noted one glide crack forming on the leeward side of Hematite with cornice fall nearby.
Multiple D3, persistent slab avalanches observed in steep, alpine terrain on N/E aspects of Circus Peak.
Observed 6 slab avalanches at upper elevations. These all appeared roughly a foot thick. We suspect these were all soft storm slabs by the looks of the debris and crown depths.
Skier-triggered slab composed of wind-drifted snow
Skier-triggered slab composed of wind-drifted snow

Observed 6 slab avalanches at upper elevations. 3 slabs were size large (D2). Few likely involved some wind-drifted snow. These all appeared roughly a foot thick. We suspect these were all soft storm slabs by the looks of the debris and crown depths.

Unsure on the timing of this avalanche. The crown looks fresher and more recent, especially when compared to other crowns I saw yesterday of avalanches that I know ran on the night of Feb. 28th/29th. Much less recent snow on the crown for this event. I'm guessing it ran earlier this week, but uncertainty is high.
An impressive avalanche. the crown width might be pushing 2000'

Multiple natural small storm snow avalanches observed breaking on specific steeper convex terrain features.  We observed both natural and remote triggered slides of this type and size. 

N aspects, 5900-6600ft, >35'

Total # of these small avalanches observed: 10+

Lakolaho Creek drainage, 6,600ft, NNW, ~35' slope

Skier remotely triggered.

Storm slab broke ~4in down. Crown was aprox 100 yards wide. Skier triggered, when skier crested rollover to  ~35' slope. 

We observed shooting cracks on an adjacent (lower angle) slope before triggering this slide.  We conducted a series of tests in a snowpit on a representative slope prior to committing to this slope. We did not observe propagation in our test pit. 

The slide was relatively harmless (not enough force to knock a skier off their feet) and broke below the skier, as they crested a convexity.

location: Below a ridge N of WMR boundary
Avalanche Summary:
Multiple small skier triggered avalanches observed on S aspects to the NW of Skook Peak.

All of these avalanches occurred within the new snow, between 6800 and 5800 feet, they were all small (D1), and they all occurred on convexities above steeper terrain features.
Storm slab failure from last night or early this AM (sunday).  Most likely failed after snowfall yesterday since crown still looked clean.

Debris from multiple large and very large natural avalanches on the basin floor at about 4000 feet. Debris from one slideoff Mt. Penrse swept down the center of the basin to about 4500 feet. Crowns from these avalanches visible on the north facing slopes of Nyack Mountain and Mt. Penrose.

location: Marion lake (from low saddle above Peanut Butter Bowl)
Avalanche Summary: Likely natural cornice failure triggered slide from saddle above Peanut Butter Bowl.  This is separate from mid February slides.  Crown extended approximately 100m lookers left and perhaps 300m lookers right across basin.  Viewed from a distance.  But crown appears perhaps 2 feet deep and likely stepped down to previously suspect facet layer.

Debris from multiple large and very large natural avalanches on the basin floor at about 4000 feet. Debris from one slideoff Mt. Penrse swept down the center of the basin to about 4500 feet. Crowns from these avalanches visible on the north facing slopes of Nyack Mountain and Mt. Penrose. The debris from the largest on Nyack was chunks of refrozen debris, with a gouged center typcial of wet snow avalanches. We suspect this slide failed late in the storm, when temperatures were warmest, and the debris entrained wet snow lower on the slope.

Debris from multiple large and very large natural avalanches on the basin floor at about 4000 feet. Debris from one slideoff Mt. Penrse swept down the center of the basin to about 4500 feet. Crowns from these avalanches visible on the north facing slopes of Nyack Mountain and Mt. Penrose. Given the few inches of snow on the debris, we estimated that most slides ran late in the cycle.
Noticed a crown across the drainage from our route on a North aspect around 5800'.

A lengthy section (100+ feet) of the cornice failed and appears to have removed a depth of 10-15 feet. The debris did not trigger a slide below the cornice, although the large blocks trundled a long way downhill. The slide was denoted as a D2 due to the large blocks, which could have injured someone.
We had a limited view of this slide and feel we did not see the full extent of the crown.
Debris was buried from recent snow, but most likely failed on thursday during warm temps on Thursday afternoon.
Persistent Slab avalanche estimated to have occurred 2/28 or 2/29. Unable to determine the vertical run due to flat light and recent snowfall.

location: Elk Mountain (South Fork of Dickey Creek)
Avalanche Summary: Hard Slab avalanche estimated to have occurred 2/28 or 2/29. Unable to determine the vertical run due to flat light and recent snowfall.

Debris from multiple large and very large natural avalanches on the basin floor at about 4000 feet. Debris from one slideoff Mt. Penrse swept down the center of the basin to about 4500 feet. Crowns from these avalanches visible on the north facing slopes of Nyack Mountain and Mt. Penrose.

location: Prominent peak southeast of Great Northern
South face just east of Standard peak.

Avalanche Summary: Reported by rider in Kimmerly Basin. Dimensions estimated on CalTopo. Not clear from image whether 1 or 2 slides. Same aspect and elevation as slide 2.25 km N reported 2/29 with known time.
Older D2 persistent slab on steep, north facing terrain in upper Castle Creek.
Saw a  couple D3 that went full path on Hay creek but they appeared to be several weeks old.
Intentionally triggered wind slab below cornice on a convex test slope.
Unintentionally-triggered slide that stepped down to deeper weak layer.

small wind slabs were relatively easy to trigger along leeward side of ridges. There isn't much snow to transport, so they do not appear to be gaining size with current wind speeds. We saw no others in the immediate area.

Big, propagated fast and far. I did not feel the slope collapse (apparently almost immediately after I started down). My partner alerted me with shouted "Right!" (SOP for us is to yell direction only). I almost made it to the left edge of the slab before the breaking snow bounced me off my feet or pulled my skis down. I tried to dig into the bed surface. When that failed, I looked downslope and saw a small tree. I attempted to grab it but hit it chest first and bounced off. In retrospect, I think that slowed me down and put me clsoer to the tail of the debris. I tumbled down hill, mostly under the snow, sometimes with some snow in my mouth. I kept fighting to stay at the surface and when the debris stopped I was. I waved to my partner and returned his yell. He was there within seconds with his beacon out. After his thorough assessment, we made the decision to self evacuate. This slide had a distinct stauchwall above a convexity to the right. The debris washed over the convexity and triggered a 2nd, similarly sized slide. The debris flowed together.

The debris from a triggered slide triggered a second slide as it ran over a convexity. The debris flowed together, though there was a distinct, seperate stuachwall above the crown of this slide. Crown thickness looked to have ranged from 20 t0 50 cm.
Observed a few recent debris piles from southerly aspects.
Small loose dry avalanche initiated in exposed cliffs due to solar input and triggered a large storm slab avalanche that ran nearly full track ~1200' vertically. SS-N-D2-R3
Several natural dry loose avalanches that occurred some time between 1:30pm and 4:00pm.
Persistent Slab avalanche that failed naturally east of Mount Geduhn.
Several natural dry loose avalanches that occurred some time between 1:30pm and 4:00pm.
Watched the slide from across the drainage. Believed to be from sun exposure.
Sometime at or around noon a tree bomb likely triggered a 50’ wide D1 wet loose at approximately 6600’ that ran for 200’ or so. We observed it from above, on the ridge.
several dry loose - maybe 3 from today and 3 from yesterday below a cliff band at approximately 6200’ on the NE aspect of Tunnel Ridge.

Skier triggered. After digging 2 pits with no resulted, and seeing no other signs of instability (besides the other avalanche we witnessed), skied a north aspect one at a time. Slope didn’t fail until 4th skier, who was able to ride out the side of the slide while all other party members below were in safe spots and untouched by the slide. Looking up from below, appeared to fail on a subtle convexity.
Was receiving ample sunlight when we witnessed this avalanche fail. Hard to judge vertical drop but best guess is 1600ft.
Observed several D1 loose snow avalanches. A copule nearing D2 size that began running after noon

Unintentionally skier triggered persistent slab avalanche that thankfully no one was caught or carried in. The avalanche was approximately 55cm deep and failed on a 3cm thick layer of small grained facets above the early February crust complex. The avalanche ran full track and onto old debris from the wet slab cycle of late January.

Two parties of 2 left the Izaak Walton trailhead planning on summitting and skiing Mt. Adams on the crest of the Flathead Range. They left at different times and were unaware of each other's plans. The first party approached across Marion Lake with a descent off the south ridge of Pt. 7798 into Essex Creek. This large avalanche path has numerous names, including Marion's Big Mouth. The second pair approached up Essex Creek.

The parties met  on the uptrack to Mt. Adams and skied the east face of the peak as a single group without incident (it had also been skied the previous day). They then climbed out of the Alameda Lake Basin in Essex Creek to the south ridge of Pt. 7798 above Marion Lake. The east-facing slope above the lake is sometimes known as Peanut Butter Bowl. It had been skied the day prior by at least two riders and by at least six riders  earlier in the day of the accident. Another party skied steeper, southeast-facing slopes to skier's left about 90 minutes before the accident. That party reported conducting and ECT that broke but did not propagate at a storm interface roughly a foot below the snow surface. 

On the ridge, the group of four split into 2 parties comprised of 1 member from each of the original groups. The two parties were equipped with radios, and avalanche rescue gear. One pair (Riders 3 and 4) planned to ski the concave, east-facing slope with existing tracks (Peanut Butter Bowl). The other pair (Riders 1 and 2) chose a line to skier's right that is northeast facing, with a steep, convex entry. 

Rider 1 skied the upper part of the slope with no incident. They stopped below the run. Rider 2 entered the slope and triggered a large, soft slab avalanche. Rider 1 alerted the rest of the group of the slide and watched Rider 2 ski off the slab to skier's left. Unfortunately, this did not give them time to escape the debris rushing down at them. The debris carried Rider 1 roughly 50 meters  through some small trees. They came to a rest mostly on the snow surface (legs buried). Rider 1 did not lose consciousness. Rider 1 was able to make brief radio contact with the rest of the group. They lost a ski and pole. The slide occurred at about 16:30. 

Riders 3 stayed on the ridge where they had cell service. Riders 2 and 4 rode down to Rider 1, where they assessed the victim and planned a rescue. They decided Rider 2 would look for the victim's lost gear while Rider 4 assessed and stabilized the victim, whose symptoms included arm, chest, and back pain. After 5 minutes or so, the group decided against self evacuating due to the back pain and alerted EMS via InReach and cell phone. 

EMS received notification at 16:50 and launched Two Bear Air. They inserted a deputy, then extracted the victim and deputy. They transferred the patient to an air ambulance at Hungry Horse Reservoir (The patient was able to walk between helicopters). The patient was hospitalized overnight and treated for serious but not life-threatening injuries. Riders 2, 3, and 4 self evacuated. 

One member of the party cited several factors that contributed to the accident:
Confirmation bias - they'd skied a larger, more consequential line earlier in the day (Mt. Adams) and there were other tracks nearby. 
Complacency and fatigue: it was late in the day, and the riders were tired. The 4 riders had already skinned over 7000 vertical feet. 

The avalanche was a large avalanche (D2) that likely failed on old snow - either an interface between storms or a thin layer of facets a few inches above the Feb. 3 ice/ crust layer.The slab may have been composed partly of wind-drifted snow. It released at roughly 7100 feet and stopped mid path. It failed on a convex, upper elevation, shaded slope. The avalanche forecast for that day and the previous days listed a Persistent Slab avalanche problem in terrain like this, with a Moderate (L2) avalanche danger. 

The party was riding one at a time, though Rider 1 did not stop in a safe zone. Their response to their accident was exemplary. 

Two other near-misses occurred the same afternoon on shaded, upper elevation slopes in the next drainage north.
Likely failed sometime after temperatures warmed on February 17th. Unknown. I suspect it failed on facets above the early December crust.
Another photo taken of the crown on 1/17. Assume this failed days prior to that. Suspect it failed on facets above the early February Crust.

location: Southeast facing slope in Skiumah drainage
Avalanche Summary: Estimated. We did not observe this slide in action.
Wet loose on the Mount Grant Headwall, likely ran 2/16 from the solar activity.
Multiple loose dry avalanches observed on steep slopes in Dickie Creek. Likely occurred earlier in the week.

location: NE chutes that funnel north from the summit of Penrose

Avalanche Summary: Storm slab avalanche on ESE face near the Grant Headwall.

Remotely triggered storm slab from the ridge.  Slab was thinner than one would expect given the amount of recent snowfall since this weekend. We speculated that it may have only involved the 2-12 overnight snowfall. Triggered from approximately 80 feet away.
storm snow appears to have failed on faceted layer above 2/3 crust

Triggered 2 large avalanches remotely on lee side of Aeneas Ridge, in adjacent chutes north of electronics site. Only minor cracking similar terrain to south, though we could not access much of it safely. Both appeared to break about a foot deep, on the new snow/ old snow interface, though we could not safely access the crown faces. We could not see how far they ran. See obs/ snowpack tab for more info about depth of nearby shooting cracks and compression test results.
Unknown timing. Estimated to have been 2/12/2024 during heightened storm intensity.
Unknown Avalanche Type. Either Storm Slab or Loose Dry. Was unable to locate definitive crowns.
Easy to trigger loose slides in 6-8" of unconsolidated snow on a stout crust.
Skier triggered soft slab 15 feet wide by 1.5 feet deep.
Multiple shallow, loose dry avalanches among the larger wet slides triggered by the abnormally warm weather in late January and early February.

We triggered a few small sluffs within the new snow but they were predictable, slow moving, and didn't travel far. We also triggered one small storm slab (SS-ASc-D1-R2 50'w x 150'l x 8"d) at 5300' NW 38* slope on our exit that broke on that broke at our feet on the old snow/new snow interface.

2/8: Easily triggered Dry Loose avalanches on very steep (>40) slopes on north facing aspects. 
We observed a handfull of old slab avalanches form the late January warmup. These likely failed as Wet Slabs.

small D1 avalanches triggered as we traveled down slopes around 35 degrees. Recent snow is bonding poorly with the stout crust layer formed last week. Faceted crystals found on crust surface on northerly aspects.
Several loose snow avalanches were releasing in the afternoon sunshine from steep rocky areas along the ridge between Elk and Little Dog. All were relatively harmless in size (D1)
We noticed multiple wet loose sluffs on steep S facing aspects in the afternoon.

location: Brown creek, north of Peter's Ridge

Avalanche Summary:
Observed one D1 natural point release avalanche that had run approximately 250 vertical feet from below the rock band on Big Slide sometime on 2/6.  SE aspect between approximately 5800' and 5550'

About 80% of Big Slide below the rocky band was raked with frozen rollerball debris from 2/6.
Wet loose avalanches from earlier in the week on steep slopes with rock outcroppings.
Several Loose Wet Slides between Brown's Pass and Hole in the Wall Cirque.
Loose wet avalanches on the west side of the Apgar Crest. Old debris visible further down the gullies from larger slides during last weeks cycle.
Wet slide from this week's warming event. Started on a W aspect above 6800'. We were unable to observe the start zone.

location: St Mary, MT East Flattop southeast face
Avalanche Summary: Slide occurred sometime after yesterday’s 2-3 inches of new snow followed by another 3 inches last night.

2/8: Easily triggered Dry Loose avalanches on very steep (>40) slopes on north facing aspects. 
We observed a handfull of old slab avalanches form the late January warmup. These likely failed as Wet Slabs.

Cornice triggered avalanche at about 7000’ on an East face, outside of the forecast area at about 1pm. Estimated to be D 1.5, crown height 2-3’ and hard to determine if it was a wind slab or PWL.

The main north path of Slippery Bill Mountain appears to have a large avalanche based on the debris we could see. Unfortunately, the visibility was limited all day, so we estimated that it was a D2.5 based on debris field. We suspect it to be a Wet Slab avalanche, but we could not confirm it. Debris field was wide. Covered upper half of most frequent slide path.
We viewed this from a distance and did not have a view of the track or runout. The vertical fall, D size, date of occurrence, and slab thickness are estimated.

The slide on Dorothy’s may have already been reported but it was likely a wet slab that broke free during the warm up of last week. It was in the skiers right bowl and the debris pile was much larger than expected. We could see a crown in the top of the bowl, and I would say the size was D 2.5-3.

location: NW facing slope of ridge off Penrose in the Skiumah drainage
Avalanche Summary: Estimated date and bed surface based on similar avalanches that failed around that time.
From WMR Patrol: "Skook Face had a wet slab pull out of the skiers right pocket sometime late morning to early afternoon."
We were expecting to trigger one of this little slabs on our up track. Lots of little test slopes to use

Large WS avalanche S of Peter's Ridge.  Approximate locations of crowns highlighted.  Appears to have stripped most of the thin snowpack in this bowl.  Precise timing of this slid unknown but likely within the last 2-3 days.

A small point-release slide traveled 10 vertical feet before initiating the avalanche. The slab initially failed at a thin rocky convexity, where the snow depth today was 30cm. As the debris traveled down the slope, a wider and deeper slab pulled out.

Relatively thin snowpack combined with yesterday's unseasonably warm temperatures and solar radiation in the east flattop are resulted in this large natural avalanche. Initial slab triggered/ stepped down in several places on ramp and apron below. Debris did not cover historic apron or run to  end of trimlines.

Wet slab avalanche involving most of Browns Bowl.  Multiple thin crowns zig-zagged through the rocky terrain.  Red line on photo a loose approximation of what I could see through the scope and in photo.
Precise timing unknown but likely within the last 2-3 days.
D2+ apparent wet loose SE aspect of penrose, toe at ~5000 ft. Crowns were observed along the path indicating penetration to persistent week later.

location: Headwall below saddle, off Nyack Mtn.
Avalanche Summary: Estimated, as fracture lines were seen through binoculars.

location: South face of Running Rabbit Mtn - Path 1163
Avalanche Summary: Debris terminated well above the rail at about 4600'

location: SE face Running Rabbit - Shed 10 Feeder
Avalanche Summary: Debris terminated at about 5300'
Wet slab avalanche released on the lookers right southwest face of Mount Stanton.
Spotted from a distance as a brown streak of debris.
Wet slab avalanche released on the lookers left southwest face of Mount Stanton. Ran to the full vertical extent of the path.
Almost every steep gully on the southeast of Mount Stanton failed during this last cycle. Many of the gullies gouged to the ground.
It appears that a WL triggered a larger WS as it hit open snow. Still relatively shallow slab thickness due to low snowpack in the start zone.
Wet slab avalanche released on the south face of Mount Cannon. The debris leapt over the massive cliff undercutting the upper face and triggered another slab avalanche in the middle of the path.
Wet slab avalanche on the south face of Mount Vaught.
Faint slabs visible on the Garden Wall. difficult to decern in the lighting of this photo. But evident when actually viewing through the spotting scope.
Wet snow avalanches on the paths to the east of West Glacier depot.

location: Canyon creek trailhead in fiber glass

Avalanche Summary: multiple small wet loose avalanches visible on southerly aspects of noisy creek.

location: North facing chute in Rescue Creek

Avalanche Summary: Multiple wet loose slides were observed in the Skook Chutes. Some of these slid on the ground, but their destructive potential was limited by the thin snowpack. It is difficult to determine if any of these are D2 because we cannot see the debris.

At least two wet slab avalanches were observed in the Skook Chutes. Both of these probably failed on buried facets resting on a crust from December. One slide is classified as D2, and the other appears to be a D1.5.

Multiple WL-triggered-WS avalanche crowns observed in the lower aprons paths past Marion lake on S and SE aspects ~6200'. Assume these ran during the wet cycle last week. Clouds rolled in obscuring better views of runouts so size is estimated.

Multiple (6 observed during ski out) WL and probably WS avalanches along Essex creek. 4 avalanches on South aspects (Essex Peak) and 2 avalanches on North aspects (from Snowshed). Could not see start zones on most paths and so unsure of crown lines but it seems as though a few had been wet slabs given the debris piles. Estimated size between D2 (4) and D3 (2) with medium diameter snapped trees in the debris. Many of the tracks above the debris piles/aprons are melted 95% out and we'd guess the avalanches cleaned out most of the season's snow in a few paths.
We can only guess where the crown might be on this slide. Based on the debris, the avalanche stepped down to the ground.
Several crowns on Swan Peak
Observed 3 D2 avlanches that likely failed into old snow. All three failed mid-slope on west facing aspects.
Multiple natural point/wet slides.
Assuming this failed on facets above 12/22 crust.

location: Brown Creek N of Strawberry Mtn.
Avalanche Summary: large avalanche that entrained most of the season's snowpack. it wrapped around several contour features and appeared to connect several rock outcroppings where snowpack was thin.
Likely ran sometime on Sunday, Jan. 28th

location: West face of Running Rabbit Mtn - I-Beam
Avalanche Summary: 3 large crowns in the path just south of Goat Lick known as I-Beam. Many details are estimated. But looks like R2-4 and D2-3 sizes with crowns ranging from 400-800' wide. This path also threaten highway 2 but associated debris terminated well above the highway. We suspect failure layer is the FCs on top the late-Dec crust.
Slide occurred between ride to upper whitefish lake and return to red meadow lake back to north fork rd.

location: FR 115 north of Red Meadow Lake
Avalanche Summary: Happened between time traveled to upper white fish lake are return to north fork rd.

location: Two miles south of red meadow lake near previous side
Avalanche Summary: Traveled from red meadow to upper whitefish lake. On return trip, observed side on return trip

location: Two miles south of Red Meadow Lake on FR 115
Avalanche Summary: Side happened between the night of 27 Jan and today. Adjacent to side reported on 25 Jan. Photo clearly shows the weak late from previous slide and reported slide. No observed crown and blocking FR 115.

location: West face of Running Rabbit Mtn - Goat Lick
Avalanche Summary: 3 separate hard slab avalanches, R2-4 and  D2-3 in size, crowns 400-600' wide. Many details are estimated at this point. Hope to investigate more this week. This path threatens Highway 2. Debris terminated well above the highway. We suspect the failure layer was the FCs on top the late-Dec crust

location: South of Red Meadow Lake on FR 115
Avalanche Summary: Traveling south of FR 115 from Red Meadow Lake to Upper Whitefish Lake. SW aspect of mountain fractures through three separate draws.  Crown of avalanche was hard to see at places but clearly was over 100m long. Debris field was not contiguous but was five separate debris fields. Conducted beacon search through the debris field and no indication of a buried individual. Didn’t see any indications of ski or snowmobile tracks leading in or exiting.

Large hard slab. Crown profile confirmed this failed on facets above the 12/22 crust. This may have been triggered by sluff, or the dead snag that was laying in the start zone with no snow on it. This appeared to have failed within the last couple of days. The snowpack at the crown was moist with wet facets/depth hoar. The crown averaged 2 to 4 feet thick.
Observed numerous D1-D1.5 wet loose avalanches. We assume these all failed during the last few days of warm temperatures and rain.

Observed a few large debris piles that came down near the river. Some terminated higher up. The upper start zones were obscured by clouds. Uncertain if these failed as slabs, or as point releases. Debris piles looked wet.

location: East path off 6996. Otherwise known as The Big Run.
Avalanche Summary: It appears to have been triggered by a skier or snowboarder in the past week.
Crown and debris mostly filled back in, but appeared to be pushing D2 size.

Thin, soft slab avalanche propagated from my bindings when I stopped just above a steep roadcut. Slab involved today's new snow. Bed surface was older, wind-hardened drift. The fracture extends out the right side of the image another 20 feet or so, and upslope. Notable propagation for such a thin slab. Perhaps a thin layer of facets sandwiched in there, though we didn't notice it.
Part of crown still visible. Could not view the runout. Looks like maybe failed on a more deeply buried weak layer?
At least a couple D1.5-2 size debris piles about halfway down the track. New snow had re-filled the crowns.

location: Old Highway 2 and the Rescue Creek summer hiking trail
Avalanche Summary: There was a thin, widespread soft slab avalanche cycle on steep slopes at low elevations. We observed these slides on steep cut banks above and below old Highway 2 and along the Rescue Creek summer trail. We assume these are storm slabs that occurred during a high-intensity precipitation event on January 17.
Tally of D2 soft slabs that failed on/near Mt. Grant and Mt. Liebig
Observed 2 recent slides with debris piles that looked to have gotten to D3.
Numerous soft slabs failed along a roadcut near the dam. These were generally small, but debris ran onto the road cut and piled up deeper.

location: Steep cut bank above the Middle Fork
Avalanche Summary: New since yesterday evening

Expected to have run sometime during the most recent storm. Generally appeared to be a dry loose avalanche, but also seemed as though there may have been a faint crown near the top of the bowl/base of the summit cliffs. Poor image quality.
At least a couple D1.5s, maybe larger on this face
Couldn't get a good view, but might be the largest crown I saw today? D2 at least. Could not clearly view the entire runout or get a good pic. Slab dimensions estimated.

location: Java Gauntlet Path near Java Creek
Avalanche Summary: Obs from Hwy around 1430. Some new snow on the crown makes me think it ran earlier this morning or last night. I did not see it yesterday. No debris on rail and did not trigger the slide fence. Sorry no pic

location: Large river cutbank above the Middle Fork
Avalanche Summary: Obs from Hwy around 1430. Some new snow on crown makes me believe it ran earlier in the morning or last night. I did not see it yesterday.
Could not see runout. Multiple shallower crowns on this face just under the ridge crest. Again, most D1-1.5s maybe one nearing D2?
Multiple crowns between 6000-7200' that were concentrated more on the west side of the path. Most were D1-1.5, but a couple probably nearing D2
A thicker slab and larger avalanche than those in Path 1163 and Shed 10. Unfortunately vis very limited from the Hwy. Could not determine runout. Crown dimensions estimated
Unknown date. Plus or minus a day. Assuming it was a hard wind slab. The avalanche did not propagate wide.
Unknown date. Plus or minus a day. Assuming a hard Wind Slab due to its location and crown width.
Unknown date. Plus or minus a day. Possibly a persistent slab. Crown propagated a relatively wide distance and wrapped around terrain features.

We observed rows of crowns down the main path of Grant. We assume these went during the pre-arctic storm due to the amount of snow on them. They appeared older than all the other avalanche activity we observed.
Stomped on a tiny cornice which pulled out a small wind slab.

Observed around daybreak from Hwy. Investigated the crown around 1600 today

location: Small cut bank just east of Marias Pass along Hwy 2
Avalanche Summary: Observed from Hwy around 0900. May have been triggered by plow debris?

Crown and debris noted from uptrack to Ghoulie Point. Tracks to skier's left (North) so unclear if triggered or natural. Given location on typical lee side of ridge, slab may have been comprised of wind-stiffened and wind-thickened snow.
Several small soft slabs on cutbanks above Beta Lakes road.
Watched a natural sluff pour off cliffs onto apron below. Did not trigger a slab.
Soft slab of new and recent snow that wrapped around the base of a rock buttress. Probably triggered by sluff from above.

Aspect Range:: N, NE, E, SE, S, SW, W, NW

Avalanche Summary: Large hard slab composed mostly of accumulated sluff debris from slopes above.  Looked to be triggered by sluff from above. Slab ranged from 60-110 cm thick at crown face. Failed about 20 cm above most recent MFcr. Weak layer not distinct - perhaps small facets buried 1/31 (just under thin, almost indistinguishable rime crust). 7-10 cm of fresh snow on debris. Guestimating time as early AM on 1/7 based on snowfall at Stahl Peak SNOTEL ~ 1 mi away.
Crown and debris from large hard slab visible on apron below steep slopes. Guessing this slab occurred about the same time as one on Barnaby and involved slab composed of sluff debris.

Wide but shallow crown visible from canyon floor. Bed surface covered with 15-20 cm recent snow. Estimating it ran during period of high snowfall intensity on Jan. 6. Weak layer not readily apparent in profile at one part of crown. Suspect mid-storm density change. Crown wrapped all the way across the bowl, including in very steep rocks above main face. Debris piles 3-6 feet deep on uphill sides of trees below. Did not hit Canyon Creek trail.  Crown deepesst on northern flank, near bottom.

Shallow crown(s) visible at convexity above skin track leading from notch to Skook. Also multiple debris piles. Fresh snow on both crowns and debris piles. Suspect soft slab failing mid storm during period of high snowfall intensity on 1/6. Debris partially covered roadbed where uptrack traverses out of the notch in piles 4-5 feet deep.
PM warming on SW faces triggered these slides on areas near rock bands.
Kicked off some sizeable sluff on a previously non-skied slope, and some more sluff skiing it a 2nd time. No sluff on run 3. No sluff on previously skied slopes.
Triggered D1 sluff while descendng steep, northerly slope. Good runout so not conesequential.
Numerous natural loose snow avalanches below cliffs. Probably ran during or shortly after snow fall ended. Perhaps with gusty winds that followed. Not obscured buy new snow.

Six natural avalanches observed in relatively short time, with largest overrunning and injuring one member of a climbing team. Probably dry loose slides, though party was well below release area. Elevation, aspect, vertical fall estimated from reports. Map location marks where party was caught, not start of slides.

Six natural avalanches observed in relatively short time, with largest overrunning and injuring one member of a climbing team. Probably dry loose slides, though party was well below release area. Elevation, aspect, vertical fall estimated from reports. Map location marks where party was caught, not start of slides. The 5 smaller slides may not have involved that much snow, but the confined, very steep terrain channeled the debris and they likely packed the destructive force of D2 slides.

Observed one crown from last Saturdays "sneaker-storm" in the alpine terrain of Paola Creek at 7200' on a NE aspect. On 12/23 Tunnel Ridge Snow recorded 11" of snow (one 3"/hour rate). Given the partially filled in crown I would estimate the release to have occurred during this storm.

D1 Wind Slab Avalanche was triggered by the second skier on the slope. Slope was a steep NW aspect on skier's left just shy of the top of Crown Bowl. The Wind Slab was not present at top of the line but was present about half way down (approx 6700-6800'). Crown depth estimated 6-12". Propagated 20-30 feet wide and a foot or two above Skier 2. While we didn't have time to assess the crown(avalanche happened at 4:15pm), we assume it was a hard wind slab on lower density snow but not on any persistent weak layer. We believe this gully to have been cross-loaded by the wind. Skier 2 was carried 5 feet, skied left and came to rest as the avalanche washed over the skier's legs and down slope. Skier 2's final position was about 20' below the crown. Snow covered to the top of the boots of skier 2 who was able to self extricate and ski the remainder of the slope.
Small loose snow avalanche visible in the Skook Chutes 1/13. Noted by WMR patrol

Two old skier tracks lead directly to the top of this avalanche. The upper portion of the slide looked like it may have began as a slab avalanche, with a few large broken pockets rather than one distinct crown line (regrettably did not take photos of the start zone). It quickly turned into a loose wet slide removing all of the recent snow since Dec 7th and gliding on a stout raincrust. This is also the only exit gully for this run, so it may just be a coincidence that the ski tracks lead directly to a fractured pocket. There is a large wall with many hanging ice features directly above this gully as well, so a natural trigger would not be surprising here. Pretty confident that this occurred some time Wednesday.
Saw a few loose dry sluffs that I am guessing occurred in the last couple days.
Debris from several wet snow slides visible in the Skook Chutes, possibly Half Moon and Big Slide

Wet debris in the bottom of Crown Bowl.  Could not see much of the track or start zone with poor visibility.  Uncertain if this initiated as point release or slab. Elevation of origin and vertical fall are estimated guesses.
Narrow and thin track leaving exposed rock.  Could not see origin or debris pile. Wet Loose or Wet Slab.
Observed two large debris piles off the crest of the Flathead. These appeared old. I suspect these failed during the warm atmospheric river in early December.

These avalanches occurred around 18-19 days ago and are adjacent to each other. The starting zone has been filled in with snow and wind-blown snow, and no crown line was visible. The debris was wet and likely triggered by rain, but it is impossible to tell if the slide was a Persistent Slab.

two natural point releases in the new snow that ran 800’.
Observed numerous wet loose slides that ran a long distance down solar faces on Great Northern. See photo.
Intentionally triggered two small wet loose avalanches. Both were harmless in size. These occurred on solar aspects in the afternoon. We observed natural roller balls prior to human triggers.
Observed a few old crowns (D1.5) on steep, unsupported northerly slopes between 6500-7500'. These crowns were about 10" thick and likely failed during the storm on Wednesday.
North facing slope had released naturally, must have run about 1000+. Debris above waterfall @5000 ft.
